concept_name: 1D Relative Motion — Same Direction vs Opposite Direction
source_book: DC Pandey 6.10 — 1D relative velocity cases; split from relative_motion

real_world_anchor.primary: Two Tata trucks on NH-44 near Jaipur. One is heading north to Delhi at 80 km/h; the other is rolling south toward Ahmedabad at 60 km/h. When they pass each other, the relative speed at which they separate is 80 + 60 = 140 km/h — a blur on both windscreens. If both were going the SAME direction at those speeds, the faster one would creep past the slower one at only 80 - 60 = 20 km/h. Same trucks, same roads — totally different relative speeds, because direction matters.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Two cyclists on a single-lane road in Gurugram. Both going north at 15 km/h — they stay exactly side-by-side forever (V_AB = 0). One turns around — now they approach each other at 30 km/h. Direction is half the physics.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Two cargo ships in the Bay of Bengal. Same direction at 20 knots and 15 knots — relative speed 5 knots. Head-on at those same speeds — relative speed 35 knots. The ocean does not change. Only the sign of one velocity changes.

formulas.same_direction: Same direction: V_AB = V_A - V_B  (relative speed = |V_A - V_B|, smaller of the two possibilities)
formulas.opposite_direction: Opposite direction: V_AB = V_A - (-V_B) = V_A + V_B  (relative speed = sum of magnitudes)
formulas.both_stationary_in_same_frame: If V_A = V_B same direction, V_AB = 0 — they appear frozen to each other.

constraints: Same direction -> subtract magnitudes -> SMALLER relative speed.
Opposite direction -> add magnitudes -> LARGER relative speed.
This follows directly from V_AB = V_A - V_B once signs are assigned.

scene_composition.text:
Same direction on NH-44:

  Truck A:  +80 km/h  (north)
  Truck B:  +60 km/h  (north)

  Pick north as positive.

  V_AB = V_A - V_B
       =  80  -  60
       =  +20 km/h

  From truck B's cabin, truck A creeps past at only 20 km/h.
  Overtaking is slow, even though ground speeds are large.
NH-44 near Jaipur: two Tata trucks going the same way

tts_sentences.text_en: Case one — two objects moving in the SAME direction.
Two Tata trucks on NH-44. Truck A at 80 kilometres per hour north, truck B at 60 kilometres per hour north.
Both are positive. V AB equals 80 minus 60 — that is only 20 kilometres per hour.
From truck B's cab, truck A crawls past at just 20. The ground speeds are large, but the relative speed is small. That is why same-direction overtakes feel sluggish.

scene_composition.text:
Opposite direction on NH-44:

  Truck A:  +80 km/h  (north, positive)
  Truck B:  -60 km/h  (south, negative)

  V_AB = V_A - V_B
       =  80  -  (-60)
       =  80  +  60
       =  +140 km/h

  Head-on approach speed is HUGE — you see both
  windscreens blur past each other in a fraction of a second.
Same two trucks, now on opposite highway sides

tts_sentences.text_en: Case two — the two objects move in OPPOSITE directions.
Truck A still at plus 80 kilometres per hour northbound. Truck B now at minus 60 — heading south.
Plug in: V AB equals 80 minus minus 60, which is 80 plus 60 — 140 kilometres per hour.
Head-on approaches are devastatingly fast. The SAME trucks, at the SAME ground speeds, have seven times the relative speed just by reversing one direction.

scene_composition.text:
One rule, two cases:

  Always:  V_AB  =  V_A  -  V_B

  Assign signs FIRST, subtract SECOND.

  Same direction (both +):     V_AB = V_A - V_B     (smaller)
  Opposite direction (one -):  V_AB = V_A + V_B     (larger)

  Common mistakes to avoid:
    X  Using |V_A| + |V_B| blindly
    X  Forgetting the minus sign when B moves the other way
    X  Treating 'direction' as an afterthought

  Discipline: sign on the PAGE first, then subtract.

tts_sentences.text_en: The beautiful thing is, there is only ONE formula.
V AB equals V A minus V B. Always. Forever.

The two cases are just different sign assignments. Same direction both positive — subtraction gives a small number. Opposite direction one negative — subtraction of a negative flips to addition and gives a large number.
The discipline is: put the sign on the page BEFORE you subtract. Never mental-math your way around this step.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student memorises 'same direction subtract, opposite direction add' without understanding WHY, and then applies it blindly even when signs are ambiguous.
confusion_patterns: when do I add velocities for relative

STATE_1.title: The Memorised Rule Fails Weird Cases

scene_composition.text:
The memorised rule:
  "Same direction -> subtract.
   Opposite direction -> add."

  Works fine in textbook problems.

  But fails when:
    * Both velocities are given as vectors with signs.
    * The problem changes sign convention mid-solution.
    * An observer's own velocity is negative.

  The reason: the rule is a shortcut, not the physics.
  The actual physics is ONE formula: V_AB = V_A - V_B,
  applied with strict signs.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the trap — memorising 'same direction subtract, opposite direction add'.
The rule works for simple problems where directions are obvious.
But it breaks when velocities arrive already signed, or when the observer has a negative velocity, or when the problem swaps direction mid-way.
That is because the rule is only a shortcut for the real physics. The real physics is one formula applied with strict signs.

STATE_2.title: The Only Formula: V_A minus V_B with Signs

scene_composition.text:
Drop the rule. Use the formula.

  Step 1: Pick a positive direction (east / north / right).
  Step 2: Write each velocity with its SIGN, no magnitudes alone.
  Step 3: Plug into V_AB = V_A - V_B.
  Step 4: The arithmetic gives the right answer.

  Example 1: V_A = +50, V_B = +30
             V_AB = 50 - 30 = +20

  Example 2: V_A = +50, V_B = -30
             V_AB = 50 - (-30) = 50 + 30 = +80

  Example 3: V_A = -50, V_B = -30
             V_AB = -50 - (-30) = -50 + 30 = -20

  Each answer is consistent with the mental picture.

tts_sentences.text_en: Drop the memorised rule. Use the formula.
Step one — pick a positive direction. Step two — write each velocity with a sign. Step three — plug into V A minus V B. Step four — let the arithmetic tell you the answer.
Three worked examples. Both positive gives plus 20. One negative gives plus 80. Both negative gives minus 20.
Notice — you never decided whether to add or subtract. The formula did the work.

STATE_3.title: Why This is Powerful — Works in 2D Too

scene_composition.text:
The same formula scales to 2D:

  Vector form:  V_AB  =  V_A  -  V_B

  If V_A = (V_Ax, V_Ay) and V_B = (V_Bx, V_By), then:

     V_AB  =  (V_Ax - V_Bx,  V_Ay - V_By)

  Subtract components. No memorisation needed.
  
  This is why the formula is called 'the law',
  not just 'the trick for 1D'.

  The add-or-subtract mnemonic cannot scale.
  V_A - V_B always does.

tts_sentences.text_en: The formula also scales to two dimensions — subtract the components.
V A minus V B works whether the motion is along a single line or all over a plane.
The shortcut rule cannot make that jump. V A minus V B always can — that is why it is the law, not just a trick for 1D.

type_a.label: Both northbound trucks, small speed gap

type_b.label: Two Mumbai locals on parallel tracks
